stem: A hospital uses an HbA1c test to screen for Type 2 diabetes. The test has a sensitivity of 91% and a specificity of 87%. The prevalence of Type 2 diabetes in the patient population is estimated to be between 1.0% and 2.4%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has Type 2 diabetes? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(Type 2 diabetes | positive test) ranges from 0.066 to 0.147 as prevalence varies from 1.0% to 2.4%. Point estimate at 1.7% prevalence: 0.108.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.13.
The confidence interval [0.066, 0.147] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (91%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
